title: How Well Do You Know Church History?
description: Thirty questions tracing major people, movements, councils and turning points in Christian history.

questions.question: Which event is traditionally associated with Pentecost in Acts 2?
options: The outpouring of the Holy Spirit and the church’s public witness
questions.explanation: Pentecost marks the Spirit’s empowering presence and the beginning of the church’s public mission.

questions.question: Why were many early Christians persecuted by Roman authorities?
options: They refused all forms of work
Their exclusive allegiance to Christ conflicted with imperial religious expectations
questions.explanation: Christian refusal to worship the emperor or other gods could be viewed as disloyal and socially disruptive.

questions.question: What was a major outcome of the Council of Nicaea in AD 325?
options: The full divinity of the Son was affirmed
questions.explanation: Nicaea confessed the Son as truly divine and of one being with the Father.

questions.question: Who was Athanasius?
options: A major defender of Nicene teaching about Christ
questions.explanation: Athanasius strongly defended the full divinity of Christ against Arian theology.

questions.question: What did the Council of Chalcedon in AD 451 teach about Christ?
options: Jesus had only a divine nature
Jesus is one person in two natures, truly divine and truly human
questions.explanation: Chalcedon confessed Christ as one person in two natures without confusion or division.

questions.question: Who is often called the father of Western monasticism?
questions.explanation: Benedict’s rule shaped Western monastic communities around prayer, work and common life.

questions.question: What is the Great Schism of 1054 commonly associated with?
options: The split between Eastern Orthodox and Western Catholic churches
The end of the Roman Empire
The formation of the Baptist movement
questions.explanation: The date symbolises the growing separation between Eastern and Western Christianity.

questions.question: What did Martin Luther challenge in 1517?
options: The sale of indulgences and wider church abuses
questions.explanation: Luther’s Ninety-Five Theses criticised indulgence preaching and helped ignite the Reformation.

questions.question: What did the phrase sola scriptura mean for many Reformers?
options: Only one Bible translation may be used
Scripture is the final norm for Christian doctrine
questions.explanation: Sola scriptura placed Scripture above other authorities while not necessarily rejecting tradition altogether.

questions.question: Who was John Calvin?
options: A major Reformation theologian based in Geneva
questions.explanation: Calvin shaped Reformed theology through his teaching, writing and ministry in Geneva.

questions.question: What was the English Reformation initially linked to?
options: Henry VIII’s break with papal authority
questions.explanation: Political and dynastic concerns under Henry VIII helped produce England’s separation from Rome.

questions.question: Who were the Anabaptists?
options: Reformers who emphasised believers’ baptism and voluntary church membership
questions.explanation: Anabaptists rejected infant baptism and stressed a gathered church of committed believers.

questions.question: Where did the modern Baptist movement emerge in the early seventeenth century?
questions.explanation: Early Baptists emerged among English Separatists, including communities connected with Amsterdam.

questions.question: What did John Wesley help lead?
questions.explanation: Wesley became a leading figure in the eighteenth-century Methodist revival.

questions.question: What was the First Great Awakening?
options: An eighteenth-century revival movement in Britain and North America
questions.explanation: The Great Awakening involved widespread evangelical preaching, conversion and renewal.

questions.question: Who was William Wilberforce?
options: A British Christian campaigner against the slave trade
questions.explanation: Wilberforce combined evangelical conviction with long political efforts against the slave trade.

questions.question: What was the Oxford Movement?
options: A movement renewing Catholic traditions within Anglicanism
questions.explanation: The nineteenth-century Oxford Movement emphasised Anglican continuity with historic catholic Christianity.

questions.question: What was a major feature of the modern missionary movement?
options: The translation of Scripture and establishment of churches across cultures
questions.explanation: Missionary activity included evangelism, Bible translation, education, medicine and church formation, though it was sometimes entangled with colonialism.

questions.question: Who was Dietrich Bonhoeffer?
options: A German pastor and theologian who resisted Nazism
questions.explanation: Bonhoeffer opposed Nazi ideology and was executed in 1945 for his involvement in resistance circles.

questions.question: What was the Barmen Declaration of 1934?
options: A defence of the church’s allegiance to Christ against Nazi control
A statement creating the Baptist denomination
questions.explanation: Barmen rejected attempts to subordinate the church’s message to Nazi ideology.

questions.question: What was Vatican II?
options: A twentieth-century Roman Catholic council of renewal and reform
questions.explanation: The Second Vatican Council, held from 1962 to 1965, addressed liturgy, Scripture, church life and engagement with the modern world.

questions.question: Where is the modern Pentecostal movement often traced in the United States?
questions.explanation: The 1906 Azusa Street Revival became a major catalyst for global Pentecostalism.

questions.question: What is the ecumenical movement?
options: An effort towards greater Christian unity and cooperation
A campaign to end Bible translation
A movement rejecting all denominations by force
questions.explanation: Ecumenism seeks dialogue, cooperation and greater visible unity among Christian traditions.

questions.question: Why is the printing press important in Christian history?
options: It enabled wider production and circulation of Bibles and theological works
questions.explanation: Printing greatly increased access to Scripture, sermons, pamphlets and theological debate.

questions.question: Who translated the Bible into German during the Reformation?
questions.explanation: Luther’s German translation strongly influenced both church life and the German language.

questions.question: Who was Augustine of Hippo?
options: An influential fourth and fifth-century theologian and bishop
questions.explanation: Augustine shaped Western theology through works on grace, the church, the Trinity and Christian life.

questions.question: What did the Council of Trent address?
options: Roman Catholic reform and responses to Protestant teaching
questions.explanation: Trent clarified Catholic doctrine and initiated reforms in response to Protestant challenges.

questions.question: What was the Social Gospel movement?
options: A movement stressing Christian engagement with social injustice and reform
questions.explanation: The Social Gospel applied Christian teaching to poverty, labour conditions and social structures.

questions.question: Why should Christians study church history?
options: To prove one tradition never made mistakes
To learn from the church’s faithfulness, failures and debates across time
questions.explanation: Church history offers perspective, humility and wisdom by showing how Christians have believed and acted in many settings.

questions.question: What is one danger in telling church history too simply?
options: It may ignore minority voices, cultural settings and the failures of powerful institutions
It makes dates easier to remember
questions.explanation: Responsible history recognises complexity, diverse voices and both faithfulness and wrongdoing.
